cards.title: ABCDE Primary Survey
cards._legacyHtml:
A — Airway: Look/listen/feel for breathing. Clear with head-tilt chin-lift (no spinal trauma) or jaw thrust (trauma). Suction if needed.
B — Breathing: Rate, depth, symmetry. O2 15 L/min via NRB mask if available. Rescue breaths if apnoeic.
C — Circulation: Carotid/radial pulse, capillary refill (<2 sec normal), bleeding control (direct pressure).
D — Disability: AVPU scale; pupil response; blood glucose (if available).
E — Exposure: Fully expose patient; look for injuries/rashes/hypothermia; maintain dignity and warmth.
Reassess ABCDE after every intervention.

cards.title: Anaphylaxis — Emergency Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Recognition: Rapid onset urticaria + angioedema + hypotension + bronchospasm after exposure to trigger.
Immediate action:
1. Call emergency services (108/112).
2. Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5 mg IM into lateral thigh (anterolateral) — or auto-injector if available. Repeat in 5 min if no improvement.
3. Lay flat with legs elevated (unless respiratory distress — sit up).
4. O2 15 L/min NRB mask.
5. IV access — 500 mL NS bolus for hypotension.
6. Chlorphenamine 10 mg IM + Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV (secondary — NOT first-line).
Do NOT delay adrenaline.

cards.title: Choking — Heimlich Manoeuvre
cards._legacyHtml:
Mild obstruction (can cough): Encourage coughing. Do not intervene manually.
Severe obstruction (cannot cough/speak/breathe):
1. 5 back blows: Lean forward, heel of hand between shoulder blades.
2. 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich): Stand behind, fist above navel below xiphoid, sharp upward inward thrust.
3. Alternate 5+5 until object expelled or patient loses consciousness.
If unconscious: CPR — each time airway opened, look for and remove visible obstruction.
Infants (<1 year): 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts (NOT abdominal thrusts).
Pregnancy/Obesity: Chest thrusts only.

cards.title: Burns Management
cards._legacyHtml:
First aid: Cool with running tap water 15–20°C for 20 minutes within 3 hours. Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste.
Classification:
  • Superficial (1°): Epidermis only — red, no blisters. Heals 7–10 days.
  • Partial thickness (2°): Epidermis + dermis — blisters, painful. Heals 14–21 days.
  • Full thickness (3°): All layers — pale/white/charred, painless. Needs grafting.
Rule of Nines (BSA): Head 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, trunk front 18%, trunk back 18%, perineum 1%.
Refer if: >10% BSA, face/hands/genitals, full thickness, chemical/electrical burns.

cards.title: Wound Care
cards._legacyHtml:
Clean wound protocol:
1. Control bleeding — direct firm pressure 5–10 min (clean cloth).
2. Irrigate wound — normal saline or clean water, high-pressure syringe irrigation to remove debris.
3. Debride — remove loose necrotic tissue with sterile forceps.
4. Apply antiseptic — Povidone-iodine 10% or chlorhexidine 0.5%.
5. Cover — non-stick sterile dressing. Change every 48–72 hours.
Tetanus prophylaxis: Booster if last dose >5 years and wound contaminated; full course if unvaccinated.
Signs of infection: Increasing redness, warmth, purulent discharge, fever — refer. Wound should NOT be tightly closed if contaminated.

cards.title: Stroke — FAST Recognition & Response
cards._legacyHtml:
FAST:
  • Face — Ask to smile; facial droop on one side?
  • Arm — Both arms raised; one drifts down?
  • Speech — Slurred, confused, unable to speak or understand?
  • Time — Call 108 immediately. Note exact time of symptom onset.
Additional symptoms: Sudden severe headache, visual loss (one eye), sudden dizziness/ataxia, sudden confusion.
Do NOT: Give food/water (risk of aspiration). Do not give aspirin until haemorrhagic stroke excluded by CT.
Window: tPA thrombolysis within 4.5 hours. Mechanical thrombectomy within 24 hours. Time = Brain.

cards.title: Febrile Seizure — Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Seizure in child 6 months – 5 years with fever (>38°C), no CNS infection or metabolic cause.
Simple FS: <15 min, generalised, single episode in 24 h — benign, no specific treatment needed.
Complex FS: >15 min, focal, multiple in 24 h — needs further investigation.
Immediate management:
1. Place on side (recovery position). Do not restrain.
2. Remove spectacles; protect from hard surfaces.
3. Time the seizure. Call 108 if >5 min.
4. DO NOT put anything in mouth.
5. Post-ictal: keep lateral, airway clear. Paracetamol for fever (not aspirin).
Rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg if seizure >5 min.

cards.title: Fracture First Aid
cards._legacyHtml:
Principles — REST: Rest, Elevate, Splint, Transfer.
Immobilisation rule: Splint the joint above AND below the fracture.
Splinting materials: SAM splint, inflatable splint, improvised (magazine, pillow).
Open fracture: Cover wound with moist sterile dressing. Do NOT push bone back. IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav) within 1 hour. Tetanus prophylaxis.
Suspected spinal fracture: Do NOT move unless airway at risk. Log-roll with 3 persons. Cervical collar + spinal board.
Neurovascular check: Check distal pulse, sensation, and movement before and after splinting and after any transport.

cards.title: Adult BLS / CPR — Step-by-Step
cards._legacyHtml:
Chain of Survival: Recognition → Activation → Early CPR → Defibrillation → Advanced care → Post-resuscitation care.
Sequence:
1. Check responsiveness (shout, tap shoulders).
2. Call 108; send someone for AED.
3. Check breathing max 10 seconds (no normal breathing = start CPR).
4. Chest compressions: Centre of chest; heel of hand; arms straight; depth 5–6 cm; rate 100–120/min; full recoil.
5. Ratio: 30 compressions : 2 breaths (or compression-only CPR if untrained/unwilling to ventilate).
6. Continue until AED arrives, advanced help takes over, or obvious signs of life return.

cards.title: Child CPR (1 year – puberty)
cards._legacyHtml:
Key differences from adult CPR:
  • 5 rescue breaths first (most paediatric arrests are respiratory in origin)
  • Compressions: one or two hands; 1/3 chest depth (~5 cm)
  • Rate: 100–120/min
  • Ratio: 15:2 (two rescuers); 30:2 (single rescuer)
Infant CPR (<1 year): 5 rescue breaths → 2-finger compressions (centre of chest, just below nipple line), depth 4 cm, 30:2 (single) or 15:2 (two).
AED use in children: Paediatric pads/mode for <8 years (<25 kg). If not available, adult AED acceptable.

cards.title: AED Use — Sequence
cards._legacyHtml:
AED Steps:
1. Power on — press button or lift lid (device will guide).
2. Apply pads — one below right clavicle (right sternal border); one lateral left chest (V6 position), mid-axillary line.
3. Stand clear — plug in cable, AED analyses rhythm. Do NOT touch patient during analysis.
4. If shock advised: announce 'Stand clear!', visually confirm no contact, press shock button.
5. Immediately resume CPR for 2 min before re-analysis.
6. If no shock advised: continue CPR for 2 min then re-analyse.
Do not delay shock to do CPR if AED is immediately available at collapse.

cards.title: Recovery Position
cards._legacyHtml:
Indications: Unresponsive patient breathing normally — to protect airway from vomit/secretion aspiration.
Steps:
1. Kneel beside patient (lying supine).
2. Near arm at right angle to body, elbow bent, palm up.
3. Far hand: bring to cheek (hold); far leg: bend knee, foot on floor.
4. Roll patient towards you onto their side (leg acts as pivot).
5. Tilt head back slightly — open airway (head-tilt chin-lift).
6. Upper knee forward to stabilise position; arm prevents rolling back.
Reassess: Breathing and pulse every 1 minute.
If turned more than 30 minutes, reposition to other side to prevent pressure injury.

cards.title: AVPU Scale
cards._legacyHtml:
A — Alert: Patient is fully awake and oriented to person, place, time, event.
V — Voice: Patient responds only to verbal stimuli (opens eyes, mumbles, moves to command).
P — Pain: Patient responds only to painful stimulus (trapezius pinch or sternal rub). No response to voice.
U — Unresponsive: No response to any stimulus.
GCS correlation: Alert ~GCS 15; Voice ~GCS 12–14; Pain ~GCS 6–11; Unresponsive ~GCS 3–5.
Use: AVPU for rapid primary survey. Use full GCS (15-point) for detailed neurological assessment and serial monitoring (head injury, ICU).

cards.title: START Triage — Mass Casualty
cards._legacyHtml:
START = Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment. 30–60 seconds per patient.
Priority categories:
  • RED (Immediate): Life-threatening but survivable. RR >30 or <10, CRT >2 sec, altered consciousness.
  • YELLOW (Delayed): Serious but stable. Can wait 1–4 hours.
  • GREEN (Minor): Walking wounded — minor injuries, can self-care.
  • BLACK (Expectant): Unsurvivable injuries given available resources, or already deceased.
Steps: (1) Direct walking patients to green area. (2) Assess remaining: open airway → breathing → circulation → mental status → tag colour.

cards.title: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
cards._legacyHtml:
ComponentResponseScore
EyeSpontaneous4
To voice3
To pain2
None1
VerbalOriented5
Confused4
Words only3
Sounds only2
None1
MotorObeys commands6
Localises pain5
Withdraws4
Flexion (decorticate)3
Extension (decerebrate)2
None1
Max: 15. Severe: ≤8. Intubate if ≤8.

cards.title: Secondary Survey — Head-to-Toe
cards._legacyHtml:
Done after ABCDE is stable.
Head/Neck: Scalp lacerations, skull deformity, periorbital/retroauricular bruising (Battle's sign, Raccoon eyes), otorrhoea/rhinorrhoea (basal skull #). Tracheal deviation, JVD, C-spine tenderness.
Chest: Paradoxical movement (flail), tracheal position, percussion (dull/hyper), auscultation, rib tenderness.
Abdomen: Distension, guarding, rigidity, Cullen's/Grey-Turner's signs (retroperitoneal bleeding).
Pelvis: Stable? (gentle lateral compression — once only).
Limbs: Deformity, crepitus, NV status (pulse, sensation, movement).
Back/Spine: Log-roll, spinous process tenderness.
